proposals.title: Beyond the Census Tract: Modeling Individual Socioeconomic Exposure Networks to Predict Child Well-Being
proposals.motivation: Decades of research, including Duncan's own work, link neighborhood poverty to adverse child outcomes in attainment and health. However, this literature overwhelmingly relies on static residential census tracts, a crude proxy that ignores the reality that individuals move through multiple environments daily. This mismatch may obscure true exposure mechanisms and limit the precision of policy interventions aimed at reducing child poverty's effects.
proposals.hypothesis: A person-centered measure of socioeconomic exposure, derived from an individual's unique network of visited locations and social contacts, will be a stronger predictor of key child outcomes (e.g., academic achievement, obesity risk) than traditional residential neighborhood poverty measures.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing longitudinal data (e.g., PSID, Add Health) with geocoded addresses and travel diaries, supplemented by new GPS mobility data from a sub-sample. Using GIS, we will construct individual activity spaces (e.g., standard deviation ellipses, daily path areas) and tag visited locations with block-group-level poverty rates. The core analytic model will calculate time-weighted exposure indices. We will employ multilevel regression to compare the predictive power of this dynamic network-based exposure against static residential poverty on outcomes, controlling for individual and household confounders.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel methodological framework for conceptualizing and measuring poverty exposure, moving the field from a place-based to a person-based paradigm. It will yield more precise estimates of environmental effects on child development and inform targeted policies (e.g., on transportation or school assignment) that address individual exposure pathways.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in directly adapting epidemiological contact-tracing and exposure network concepts to socioeconomic research, challenging the fundamental assumption that the residential neighborhood is the primary or sole relevant spatial context for poverty effects.

proposals.title: Resident Outcomes as Drivers of Neighborhood Change: A Causal Test of Reciprocal Pathways in Urban Ecology
proposals.motivation: A core debate in urban sociology and child development concerns the extent to which neighborhood characteristics are shaped by the attributes and behaviors of their residents versus by larger structural forces like policy and economic shifts. This distinction is critical for designing effective anti-poverty and place-based interventions, yet existing observational studies struggle to separate these pathways, often conflating resident-driven change with structural effects.
proposals.hypothesis: Exogenous shocks that alter the socioeconomic status or health of a neighborhood's residents—independent of prior neighborhood conditions—will cause measurable, subsequent changes in neighborhood-level outcomes, including social cohesion, local service quality, and property maintenance.
proposals.method: We will construct a longitudinal panel of U.S. neighborhoods using multi-source data (American Community Survey, health surveillance systems, municipal property records, and policy databases). We will identify plausibly exogenous shocks, such as the sudden opening or closure of a major employer or a targeted housing voucher lottery, that shift resident composition or well-being. Using a difference-in-differences framework combined with entropy balancing for covariate adjustment, we will compare the post-shock trajectories of 'treated' neighborhoods to statistically matched counterfactual neighborhoods, estimating the causal effect of the resident-based shock on ecological outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the first rigorous, causal estimates of the 'resident effects' pathway in neighborhood ecology. It will directly inform debates on the efficacy of resident-focused versus place-focused policies for improving child and family well-being. The findings will offer a methodological template for disentangling agency and structure in spatial research.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Duncan and others has established neighborhood effects on individuals, this study reverses the causal arrow in a methodologically robust way, using exogenous shocks to isolate the influence of residents on their neighborhood's ecology, a pathway often assumed but rarely tested with causal designs.

proposals.title: A Dynamic Systems Model for Optimizing Early Childhood Investments and Neighborhood Social Capital
proposals.motivation: Decades of research, including seminal work by Duncan and others, show that early childhood interventions can improve child outcomes, yet these gains often fade and fail to scale. This is partly because programs are typically evaluated in isolation, ignoring their potential reciprocal relationship with the neighborhood environment. A pre-2026 policy priority is reducing child poverty and improving child well-being through efficient, synergistic investments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an optimal, dynamic allocation of resources between preschool and home-visiting services will not only maximize immediate child school readiness but also, through the aggregation of improved family functioning, enhance neighborhood-level social networks and collective efficacy, creating a positive feedback loop that sustains long-term gains.
proposals.method: We will develop a coupled system dynamics model with two core components: (1) an agent-based model of families receiving services, parameterized with pre-2026 data (e.g., Early Childhood Longitudinal Study, Head Start Impact Study), and (2) a neighborhood ecology module representing social network density and norms. Using optimal control theory and reinforcement learning algorithms (specifically, a model-based policy optimization approach), we will solve for the resource allocation policy that maximizes a dual-objective function combining child attainment and neighborhood social capital over a 10-year horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable framework for policymakers to move beyond evaluating single programs toward optimizing portfolios of early childhood services. It will formally model the theorized, but rarely quantified, community-level spillovers of family-focused interventions, directly informing initiatives like those in the 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' report.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines program efficacy or neighborhood effects separately, this model treats them as a dynamically coupled system and uses optimization techniques from engineering to solve for investment strategies that explicitly target the feedback loop between service outcomes and community ecology.

proposals.title: The Child as a Driver of Neighborhood Change: A Longitudinal Analysis of How Educational Attainment and Family Well-Being Shape Community Socioeconomic Status
proposals.motivation: Extensive pre-2026 research, including Duncan's own work, documents how neighborhood poverty and disadvantage influence child outcomes like educational attainment and obesity. However, this ecological influence is largely conceptualized as unidirectional. This proposal inverts this causal arrow, investigating how the aggregated outcomes of children and families may reciprocally shape the socioeconomic characteristics of the neighborhoods they inhabit over time, a critical but understudied feedback loop in the dynamics of poverty and place.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that improvements in the aggregate educational attainment and family economic well-being of a neighborhood's resident children will predict subsequent increases in that neighborhood's socioeconomic status (SES), as measured by rising home values, income levels, and business investment, net of initial neighborhood conditions.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal, geocoded data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development Supplement (CDS), tracking cohorts from childhood into adulthood. Using a cross-lagged panel model (CLPM) framework, we will estimate reciprocal effects between neighborhood SES (census-tract level indices) and aggregated child/family outcomes (e.g., high school completion rates, household income-to-poverty ratios, parental employment) across multiple waves. The analysis will control for time-invariant confounders using neighborhood fixed effects and for time-varying confounders (e.g., city-wide economic shocks) using propensity score stratification within a structural equation modeling approach.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, longitudinal evidence for child-driven neighborhood change, challenging purely structuralist models of community development. It will contribute to a more dynamic, reciprocal theory of ecological influence, with implications for policies aimed at breaking intergenerational cycles of poverty by highlighting the potential for investments in children to catalyze broader community revitalization.
proposals.novelty: While the influence of neighborhood on child is well-established, empirically testing the reverse pathway—from the developmental and economic trajectories of a cohort of children to macro-level neighborhood characteristics—represents a significant inversion of standard analytical models in developmental and sociological research.

proposals.title: Causal Pathways from Social Cohesion to Child Well-being: A Randomized Trial and Longitudinal Observational Study
proposals.motivation: Decades of research, including seminal work like the Moving to Opportunity experiment, demonstrate that neighborhoods influence child development and long-term attainment. However, the specific causal mechanisms—such as social cohesion—remain poorly identified, limiting the design of effective place-based policies. This gap is critical as persistent child poverty and widening health disparities, including childhood obesity, demand interventions with clear theoretical grounding and causal evidence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a randomized community-building intervention designed to enhance neighborhood social cohesion will causally improve resident mental health and parental stress, and that these proximal improvements will mediate downstream gains in children's socio-emotional well-being and educational engagement.
proposals.method: The NMCP Initiative employs a hybrid experimental-observational design. First, we will randomize 50 low-income neighborhoods to receive a structured community-building program (e.g., facilitated block meetings, shared project grants) or serve as controls. Second, we will embed this trial within a longitudinal observational study of 200 matched neighborhoods, tracking families via annual surveys and administrative records on health, education, and income. We will use multi-level structural equation modeling (SEM) to estimate the direct and mediated effects of the intervention, and leverage the observational data with techniques like propensity score stratification to examine heterogeneity of effects by family poverty depth and duration.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first experimental evidence directly testing social cohesion as a causal lever for improving child well-being, moving beyond correlational findings. It will yield a detailed map of how neighborhood mechanisms operate, informing more targeted and cost-effective policies for reducing child poverty and its associated adversities in health and education.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has documented neighborhood correlations, this study's pre-registered, mechanism-focused randomized trial nested within a broader longitudinal design is novel. It directly isolates a specific causal pathway (cohesion→mental health→child outcomes) while simultaneously assessing the generalizability and heterogeneity of that pathway across diverse community contexts.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Neighborhood Effects on Child and Adolescent Development: A Longitudinal Study of Poverty, Educational Attainment, and Well-Being
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of research, the causal mechanisms linking neighborhood context to child development remain inadequately understood, particularly regarding how timing, duration, and specific neighborhood characteristics differentially impact key outcomes like educational attainment and obesity. This gap limits the design of effective place-based policies to reduce child poverty and improve life chances. A formal, long-term research program is needed to move beyond cross-sectional correlations and identify actionable levers for intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained exposure to high-opportunity neighborhoods during specific developmental windows (e.g., early childhood, adolescence) will have stronger causal effects on long-term educational attainment and physical health (e.g., lower obesity rates) than exposure during other periods, and that these effects are mediated by changes in family processes, access to resources, and child well-being.
proposals.method: We propose establishing a new longitudinal cohort study, building on methods from the Moving to Opportunity experiment and Panel Study of Income Dynamics. We will recruit a diverse sample of 3,000 families with young children, collecting annual data on neighborhood characteristics (using GIS-linked administrative data on poverty, school quality, green space, food environment), family economic circumstances, parenting practices, child well-being, educational progress, and health metrics. We will employ dynamic structural equation modeling (DSEM) and marginal structural models with time-varying exposures and confounders to estimate causal effects of neighborhood quality across developmental stages, rigorously controlling for confounding as emphasized in Duncan's prior work on construct clarity.
proposals.contribution: This program will provide the field with a high-resolution, longitudinal dataset specifically designed to test causal hypotheses about neighborhood effects. It will generate novel evidence on the critical timing of neighborhood influences, informing more targeted housing and community investment policies. The findings will directly contribute to the national 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' by evaluating a key pathway through which poverty operates.
proposals.novelty: While many studies observe neighborhood correlations, this program's novelty lies in its explicit focus on developmental timing and its integration of multiple outcome domains (education, health, well-being) within a single causal modeling framework from inception, allowing for direct tests of competing mediating pathways over time.

proposals.title: The Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Neighborhood Effects on Child Development: A Longitudinal Spatial Econometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Decades of research, including seminal work by Duncan and colleagues, demonstrate that neighborhood poverty significantly impacts child educational attainment and well-being. However, most studies rely on static or short-term measures, failing to capture how neighborhood effects evolve over time and spill over administrative boundaries. This limits our understanding of the dynamic processes through which neighborhood disinvestment, crime, or social capital diffusion shape life trajectories, hindering the design of effective place-based policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the influence of neighborhood characteristics on child outcomes operates through dynamic spatial spillovers, where changes in economic conditions and social processes in one census tract have lagged and geographically diffuse effects on outcomes in adjacent tracts.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel longitudinal panel by linking individual-level data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development Supplement to decennial census and American Community Survey data, creating a geocoded panel of children and neighborhoods from 2000-2025. We will employ a dynamic spatial Durbin model (SDM) with fixed effects, using maximum likelihood estimation, to disentangle endogenous spatial spillovers, exogenous contextual effects, and temporal lags. This model will explicitly test for the impact of a tract's lagged poverty rate and the contemporaneous and lagged poverty rates of its neighbors on child BMI and academic achievement.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide the first formal, longitudinal spatial econometric evidence on how neighborhood effects propagate and persist, moving beyond static correlations. It will directly inform place-based anti-poverty policy by quantifying the likely spatial and temporal reach of interventions, such as housing vouchers or community investment programs, on child development outcomes in targeted and surrounding areas.
proposals.novelty: While spatial econometrics is common in geography and economics, its application to longitudinal, individual-level developmental data is novel. The core innovation is the unified modeling of both the persistent spatial structure of inequality and the dynamic, lagged pathways of influence, which existing neighborhood effects literature has not achieved.

proposals.title: The Dynamics of Social Ties and Resource Flows: A Mixed-Methods Study of How Relational Properties Mediate Support in High- and Low-Poverty Neighborhoods
proposals.motivation: Understanding the mechanisms through which neighborhood poverty affects child and family well-being requires moving beyond static structural measures. Pre-2026 research, including Duncan's work on poverty and attainment, highlights the importance of social capital and resource access, but often lacks dynamic, causal evidence on how specific properties of social ties (e.g., trust, reciprocity) actually enable the flow of critical resources like childcare, job information, or emergency aid. This gap limits the design of effective community-level interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: The activation of social ties for resource exchange is causally predicted by the dynamic interplay of relational properties—specifically, tie strength, multiplexity, and perceived trustworthiness—and these mechanisms function differently in high- versus low-poverty neighborhoods, particularly in response to economic or health shocks.
proposals.method: We will implement a 3-wave longitudinal panel study in two contrasting urban neighborhoods. Quantitative social network surveys will map ego-centric networks and measure tie properties using validated scales. Embedded qualitative modules will include structured interviews on tie history and content, and resource mobilization diaries to log actual exchanges. Analysis will integrate methods: dynamic network models (e.g., Stochastic Actor-Oriented Models using RSiena) will test for causal influence of relational properties on exchange patterns, while thematic analysis of qualitative data will explain the mechanisms behind these statistical relationships, especially during shock periods.
proposals.contribution: This research will provide a novel, integrated causal model linking the microstructure of social relations to macro-level resource disparities, directly informing policies aimed at strengthening community resilience. It will advance the science of poverty and child development by explaining not just who has ties, but how and when those ties become functionally activated for support.
proposals.novelty: While prior work maps network structure or correlates social capital with outcomes, this study's integration of longitudinal network modeling with real-time, qualitative data on exchange mechanisms to establish causality within and between neighborhoods is a significant methodological and conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Sequences of Place: A Life-Course Event History Analysis of Neighborhood Exposure and the Evolution of the Academic Achievement Gap from Early Childhood to High School
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of research on neighborhood effects, most studies treat exposure as a static snapshot, failing to capture the dynamic sequences of contexts children actually experience. This is a critical gap, as residential mobility and changing neighborhood socioeconomic status (SES) are common, especially for low-income families. Understanding how the timing, order, and duration of these exposures shape academic trajectories is essential for designing effective, temporally sensitive housing and education policies aimed at reducing persistent achievement gaps.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sequences of neighborhood exposure, modeled as time-varying processes, have distinct effects on academic trajectories, with sensitive periods in early and middle childhood where changes in neighborhood SES are particularly consequential for long-term achievement. Further, we hypothesize that cumulative exposure to high-SES neighborhoods, even if non-consecutive, is more predictive of sustained academic gains than single-point-in-time measures.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal data (e.g., Panel Study of Income Dynamics, Child Development Supplement) tracking children from birth through adolescence. Using sequence analysis, we will characterize typologies of neighborhood exposure sequences (e.g., stable high-SES, upward/downward mobility, chronic poverty). Discrete-time event history analysis (survival analysis) will model the timing of key educational transitions (e.g., falling behind grade level in reading). We will employ time-varying covariates for neighborhood SES and fixed-effects models to control for time-invariant family heterogeneity, testing for period-specific effects of neighborhood quality on achievement growth curves.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field beyond static 'dosage' models by providing a dynamic, life-course framework for neighborhood effects. It will identify whether there are critical or sensitive developmental periods for neighborhood interventions and clarify how residential instability interacts with neighborhood quality. The findings will offer nuanced evidence for policymakers on the potential long-term educational benefits of housing mobility programs and place-based investments timed to specific developmental stages.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing demographic life-course methods (sequence and event history analysis) with developmental and educational research to model neighborhood exposure as a time-varying process, directly testing for sensitive periods. This moves beyond correlating a single neighborhood measure with an outcome to modeling how the *sequence* of contexts dynamically influences the *evolution* of the achievement gap itself.

proposals.title: The Other Side of Segregation: Mechanisms and Consequences of Concentrated Affluence for Child and Adolescent Well-Being
proposals.motivation: Extensive research, including Duncan's own work, has documented the detrimental effects of concentrated poverty on child development and educational attainment. However, the parallel phenomenon of concentrated affluence—where high-income families cluster in exclusive neighborhoods and school districts—remains understudied as a structural force shaping inequality. Understanding this 'dual problem' is critical in a pre-2026 context of rising income segregation, as it may perpetuate advantage through distinct mechanisms with societal consequences.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that concentrated affluence operates through distinct ecological mechanisms—including competitive pressure, attenuated social ties, and institutional resource hoarding—that generate non-linear effects on child and adolescent well-being, potentially increasing risks for internalizing disorders, substance use, and unhealthy weight-related behaviors, even while boosting academic attainment.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal data (e.g., Panel Study of Income Dynamics, Add Health) to construct dynamic measures of neighborhood and school-district affluence concentration. Using fixed-effects and sibling-difference models to control for family-level confounding, we will estimate the causal impact of exposure to concentrated affluence on a range of outcomes. The analysis will employ causal mediation analysis with machine learning (e.g., Double Machine Learning) to formally test hypothesized pathways, such as peer norms, parental anxiety, and school spending inequality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, causal evidence on how concentrated affluence functions as an independent driver of inequality in child development. It will shift the policy discourse beyond poverty deconcentration to consider the full spectrum of residential segregation. The findings will inform interventions aimed at reducing opportunity hoarding and promoting healthier developmental contexts across the socioeconomic spectrum.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often treats affluence as a linear protective factor, this study theorizes it as a potentially detrimental ecological context at high concentrations. It applies rigorous causal methods, developed for studying poverty, to the under-examined 'upper tail' of segregation, testing for specific social-psychological and institutional mechanisms beyond simple resource availability.

proposals.title: Community Capital and Adolescent Well-being: A Strengths-Based Analysis of Resilience Among Minority Youth
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on minority youth mental health and educational attainment is predominantly deficit-focused, emphasizing poverty, discrimination, and risk factors. While vital, this perspective can obscure the protective community strengths that foster resilience and positive development. A rigorous, strengths-based analysis is needed to identify actionable community assets that policymakers and practitioners can support.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, measurable forms of community capital—such as social cohesion, institutional trust, and cultural connectedness—significantly mediate the relationship between socioeconomic disadvantage and key youth outcomes, including mental health, educational engagement, and healthy behaviors.
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, longitudinal panel data (e.g., the Panel Study of Income Dynamics’ Child Development Supplement, the National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health) to construct latent variables for community strengths. Using structural equation modeling (SEM) with a cross-lagged panel design, we will test mediation pathways. Analyses will control for individual and family-level confounders (e.g., household income, parental education) to isolate the effect of community-level constructs, following rigorous methods for construct clarity and confounding control from Duncan's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide quantitative evidence for community strengths as levers for improving youth well-being, moving beyond cataloging deficits. It will offer a novel analytical framework for strengths-based research and deliver specific, community-level factors that can inform asset-based interventions and policies aimed at reducing disparities.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard deficit model by quantitatively modeling community strengths as primary explanatory variables for positive outcomes, rather than solely controlling for risk. It integrates Duncan's expertise in poverty and developmental research with a positive deviance framework rarely applied in large-scale econometric analyses of child development.

proposals.title: Racial and Ethnic Disparities in Youth Mental Health and Service Use: The Mediating Roles of Poverty and Neighborhood Context
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research documents significant racial and ethnic disparities in youth mental health outcomes and access to care, but the pathways linking structural inequities to these disparities are not fully understood. This gap is critical, as the COVID-19 pandemic has exacerbated youth mental health crises and likely widened existing gaps in service utilization. Understanding the specific mechanisms is essential for designing targeted policies to improve child well-being and educational attainment, core themes of Duncan's work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observed racial/ethnic disparities in youth internalizing and externalizing symptoms, as well as in mental health service use, are significantly mediated by differential exposure to childhood poverty and by measurable differences in neighborhood constructs (e.g., collective efficacy, disorder).
proposals.method: We will leverage large-scale, longitudinal panel data (e.g., Panel Study of Income Dynamics - Child Development Supplement, Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study) tracking children from early childhood through adolescence. Using a causal mediation analysis framework, we will employ a regression-based approach with inverse probability weighting to account for time-varying confounders. The analysis will formally decompose the total effect of race/ethnicity on mental health outcomes into direct and indirect effects operating through time-averaged poverty exposure and time-varying neighborhood quality measures.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting disparities to quantify the proportion explained by modifiable economic and environmental factors, directly informing the policy roadmap for reducing child poverty. It will provide concrete evidence on whether equalizing economic resources and neighborhood conditions could reduce mental health gaps, contributing to the literature on child well-being and the long-term impacts of early adversity on educational and life outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links poverty to mental health, this proposal uniquely integrates detailed neighborhood constructs into a formal mediation model to explain racial disparities, and explicitly connects service use disparities to these same pathways, offering a more comprehensive systems-level analysis.

proposals.title: The Micro-Geography of Adversity: Block-Level Neighborhood Characteristics and Adolescent Mental Health and Service Utilization
proposals.motivation: Prior research on neighborhood effects, including Duncan's work on poverty and attainment, often relies on coarse geographic units like ZIP codes or census tracts, potentially masking critical hyper-local variation. The immediate, daily environments of children and young people—their blocks—may exert unique influences on psychological well-being and help-seeking behaviors that broader area studies miss. Understanding these micro-contexts is crucial for designing precise, place-based interventions to support youth mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that block-level characteristics (e.g., property maintenance, vacant lots, street-level social cohesion) will predict adolescent mental health symptoms and mental health service utilization more strongly than tract-level aggregates, even after controlling for family-level poverty and individual characteristics.
proposals.method: We will leverage high-resolution, longitudinal data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development and Transition to Adulthood supplements, merged with micro-geographic data from sources like the National Neighborhood Data Archive (NaNDA) and Google Street View imagery. Using multilevel hierarchical linear models, we will nest individuals within blocks and blocks within tracts. Key independent variables will be constructed from computer vision algorithms (e.g., convolutional neural networks) applied to street view imagery to objectively assess physical disorder and green space at the block face. Analyses will control for family income, parental education, and prior child well-being.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first nationally representative, longitudinal evidence on the mental health impacts of micro-neighborhood environments. It will advance methodological precision in neighborhood effects research by moving beyond administrative boundaries. The findings will directly inform hyper-local policy levers, such as blight remediation or micro-place greening programs, aimed at improving child and adolescent well-being.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate analytical shift from tract to block-level effects and the integration of computer vision for scalable, objective measurement of the immediate built environment, a granularity rarely achieved in large-scale developmental studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: The Geography of Youth Opportunity: A National Study of Service Provision Disparities in High-Minority Communities
proposals.motivation: Extensive pre-2026 research, including Duncan's work on poverty and child well-being, demonstrates that neighborhood context profoundly shapes developmental outcomes. However, studies often focus on individual or family-level mediators of disadvantage, rather than systematically mapping the inequitable distribution of the public and private services that constitute opportunity structures. Understanding the specific quality and type deficits in areas with high concentrations of minority youth is critical for moving beyond income-based poverty measures to target service investments effectively.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that communities with higher proportions of minority youth will exhibit systematically lower quality and less diverse provision of key developmental services—including early childhood education, extracurricular activities, and mental health supports—even after controlling for community income and poverty rates.
proposals.method: We will construct a novel national geospatial database linking (1) restricted-access American Community Survey data (2015-2019) on demographic and economic structure, (2) administrative data on service providers (e.g., Head Start, public parks, community health centers), and (3) crowd-sourced quality metrics (e.g., Yelp ratings, GreatSchools scores). Using multilevel Bayesian regression models, we will estimate the relationship between community minority youth concentration and service indices, controlling for confounders. Spatial error models will account for autocorrelation, and propensity score matching will test robustness.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, national audit of service provision disparities facing minority youth, quantifying gaps beyond simple poverty metrics. It will directly inform policy recommendations in reports like 'A Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' by identifying specific service domains for targeted investment. The geospatial database will also serve as a public resource for future research on neighborhood effects.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links poverty to service access, this study uniquely focuses on minority concentration as a potential independent driver of disparity, and innovates by integrating traditional administrative data with novel digital trace data to measure service *quality*, not just presence.

proposals.title: Distinct Pathways or a Common Vulnerability? A Latent Variable Analysis of Economic Deprivation's Impact on Child Cognitive and Behavioral Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on childhood poverty often examines cognitive (e.g., academic attainment) and behavioral (e.g., self-regulation, conduct) outcomes separately, implicitly treating them as distinct domains. However, theories of allostatic load and cumulative risk suggest economic deprivation may create a general latent vulnerability affecting multiple developmental systems. Clarifying this structure is crucial for designing effective, targeted interventions to improve child well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between early childhood economic deprivation and later cognitive and behavioral outcomes is primarily mediated by a single, general latent factor of developmental vulnerability, rather than by distinct, domain-specific pathways.
proposals.method: We will analyze longitudinal data (e.g., from the NICHD SECCYD or ECLS-K) using structural equation modeling. First, we will construct latent variables for economic deprivation (using indicators of income, material hardship, and neighborhood poverty) and for child outcomes (cognitive skills and behavioral functioning). We will then test and compare two competing models: a single-factor mediation model where deprivation influences a general vulnerability factor, which in turn affects both domains, and a two-pathway model where deprivation has distinct direct effects on each domain. Model fit will be evaluated using CFI, RMSEA, and chi-square difference tests.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous empirical test of the 'general vulnerability' hypothesis versus domain-specific models. It will advance methodological clarity in developmental economics by formally modeling the construct of deprivation's impact. The findings will inform whether anti-poverty policies should aim to bolster general resilience or require domain-specific supports.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Duncan and others has established links between poverty and both attainment and behavior, explicitly modeling their shared versus unique etiological pathways using latent variable techniques is a novel integration of developmental and econometric methods to address a core theoretical question.

proposals.title: The Timing of Early Deprivation: A Quasi-Experimental Comparison of Onset in Infancy versus Later Childhood on Educational Attainment and Health
proposals.motivation: Research on child poverty, including the 2019 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' report, establishes that early deprivation harms later-life outcomes. However, the specific developmental timing of deprivation onset—particularly whether the first year of life is uniquely sensitive—remains a critical, policy-relevant question. Determining the most critical period can inform the targeting of early interventions like cash transfers or nutritional support to maximize their impact on long-term attainment and health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the onset of significant material deprivation in the first year of life (infancy) will have a stronger negative association with later educational attainment and metabolic health indicators (e.g., BMI, obesity risk) than the onset of deprivation at ages 2-5, even after controlling for the duration and depth of poverty.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal data from the Fragile Families and Child Wellbeing Study, applying a propensity score matching framework to create comparable groups of children who experienced poverty onset at different ages (0-1 vs. 2-5). The primary analytical model will be a difference-in-differences specification, comparing the trajectories of key outcomes (standardized test scores, grade repetition, BMI z-scores) from age 5 through adolescence. We will employ inverse probability of treatment weighting to adjust for pre-onset confounding variables (e.g., maternal education, birth weight, family structure) and conduct sensitivity analyses using Lee bounds to assess robustness to potential hidden bias.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide rigorous, quasi-experimental evidence on the developmental timing of poverty effects, directly informing debates on the 'infant sensitivity' hypothesis. It will contribute to Duncan's body of work on poverty and child well-being by isolating the age-of-onset effect from cumulative exposure. The findings will offer concrete evidence for policymakers on the potential returns of interventions specifically targeted in the first year of life.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines cumulative poverty or early childhood generally, this proposal's novelty lies in its explicit, quasi-experimental comparison of deprivation onset in infancy versus later early childhood, treating timing as a distinct causal construct rather than a proxy for duration.

proposals.title: The Persistence of Early-Life Associations: A Longitudinal Study of Poverty, Self-Regulation, and Educational Attainment from Early Childhood to Adolescence
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Duncan's work on child poverty and the 'marshmallow test' debate, has established significant associations between early childhood poverty, self-regulation, and later outcomes. However, a critical gap exists in understanding whether these early associations persist, fade, or transform as children navigate key developmental transitions into school age and adolescence. This is essential for determining the optimal timing and targeting of policy interventions aimed at improving long-term educational attainment and well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the association between early childhood poverty and later educational attainment is partially, but not fully, mediated by early self-regulation skills, and that this mediating pathway weakens as children age, with school-age academic skills and adolescent social contexts becoming increasingly salient mediators.
proposals.method: We will leverage high-quality longitudinal datasets (e.g., the NICHD Study of Early Child Care and Youth Development, the Fragile Families and Child Wellbeing Study) with repeated measures from age 3 through adolescence. Using a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework, we will implement a longitudinal mediation model with latent variables for self-regulation. Key analyses will include cross-lagged panel models to test directional influences and time-varying effect modification to assess how the strength of the poverty-attainment association changes across developmental stages, rigorously controlling for time-invariant and time-varying confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, developmental map of how early disadvantages propagate over time, directly informing the 'when' and 'how' of interventions from Duncan's 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty.' It will advance methodological clarity in life-course research by explicitly modeling how mediating constructs and their effects evolve, moving beyond static, point-in-time assessments.
proposals.novelty: While many studies link early poverty to later outcomes, this proposal uniquely tests the dynamic nature of the mediating pathway through self-regulation across distinct developmental periods, challenging the assumption that early childhood is a uniquely sensitive period for all pathways to attainment.

proposals.title: Targeting Parental Stress and Cognitive Bandwidth: A Randomized Evaluation of a Light-Touch Intervention to Mitigate the Impact of Material Deprivation on Early Childhood Development
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Duncan's own work, robustly links early childhood poverty to adverse outcomes in educational attainment and health, including obesity. A key hypothesized pathway is that material deprivation increases parental stress and reduces cognitive bandwidth, thereby compromising parenting quality and child development. However, few interventions directly and experimentally test this specific mediating mechanism.
proposals.hypothesis: A low-cost intervention designed to reduce parental stress and free up cognitive resources for parents in poverty will improve observed parenting behaviors (e.g., warmth, consistency), which in turn will lead to measurable gains in children's cognitive and socio-emotional development.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-site randomized controlled trial (RCT) with 500 low-income families with children aged 2-4. The treatment group receives a bundled intervention: 1) a behavioral nudges program (via text) to reduce administrative burden and plan parenting activities, and 2) brief, structured group sessions teaching stress-regulation techniques. We will measure the primary mediator (parenting stress/bandwidth via surveys and behavioral tasks) and secondary mediators (observed parenting quality) at 6 months. Child outcomes (executive function, pre-academic skills, BMI) will be assessed at 12 months. Analysis will use intent-to-treat estimation and causal mediation analysis (e.g., using the methods of Imai, Keele, and Yamamoto, 2010) to formally test the parenting pathway.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rigorous experimental test of a core theoretical pathway in the intergenerational transmission of poverty. It will generate evidence on whether a scalable, light-touch program that targets parental psychology can disrupt the link between deprivation and child development, offering a potential model for cost-effective policy.
proposals.novelty: While many parenting programs exist, this proposal uniquely combines a bandwidth-liberation component with stress reduction explicitly to test their combined role as a mediator, moving beyond simply correlating poverty with outcomes or evaluating comprehensive, expensive home-visiting models.

proposals.title: Estimating the Causal Effect of Family Income on Child Educational Attainment and Health: An Instrumental Variables Analysis Using Policy-Induced Income Shocks
proposals.motivation: Despite strong correlational evidence linking family income to child outcomes, establishing causality remains a critical challenge due to unobserved parental characteristics. This impedes the design of effective anti-poverty policies. Pre-2026 research, including Duncan's work on child poverty, highlights the need for rigorous causal estimates to inform investments in children's development and long-term well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: Exogenous increases in family income, instrumented by policy-driven income transfers, cause significant improvements in children's educational attainment and reductions in childhood obesity, with effects varying by the developmental stage at which the income boost occurs.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage least squares (2SLS) instrumental variables framework. The primary instrument will be the simulated generosity of state-level Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) expansions, exploiting policy variation across states and over time. We will analyze longitudinal data (e.g., PSID, NLSY) to model child outcomes (test scores, grade progression, BMI) as a function of family income, instrumented by the policy variable, while controlling for state and year fixed effects, demographic characteristics, and baseline economic conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide some of the most credible quasi-experimental estimates of the causal impact of income on two key domains of child development: educational attainment and physical health. It will directly inform the cost-benefit analyses of income support policies like the EITC and Child Tax Credit, offering evidence on their potential to reduce intergenerational inequality.
proposals.novelty: While IV methods have been used, this proposal uniquely integrates Duncan's expertise on poverty, child development, and causal inference to jointly model educational and health outcomes, testing for sensitive period effects by instrumenting income at different stages of childhood.

proposals.title: Comparative Welfare Regimes and Child Development: A Cross-National Analysis of Policy Levers on the Deprivation-Attainment Pathway
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Duncan's work on child poverty, establishes strong links between early deprivation and later-life outcomes in educational attainment and health, such as obesity. However, most evidence is from single-country studies, primarily the U.S., limiting understanding of how different national policy architectures can mitigate these pathways. A cross-national comparison is critical to identify which welfare state features most effectively disrupt the cycle of poverty and poor developmental outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Nations with more generous, universal, and early-childhood-focused welfare policies (e.g., Nordic models) will exhibit a weaker statistical association between early-life economic deprivation and subsequent adverse outcomes in educational attainment and adolescent obesity, compared to nations with more residual, means-tested systems (e.g., Anglo-Saxon models).
proposals.method: We will employ a harmonized, longitudinal analysis of existing cohort studies from at least 6 OECD nations (e.g., U.S. PSID, UK Millennium Cohort, Danish registers). The core method is a multi-group structural equation model (SEM) framework. We will model the deprivation-development pathway, where early childhood poverty (construct using income-to-needs ratios) predicts latent constructs of adolescent well-being (encompassing standardized test scores and BMI). National policy regimes, characterized via latent class analysis of OECD SOCX and family policy data, will be included as moderators of the structural paths, testing for cross-national heterogeneity in effect sizes.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting U.S.-specific associations to provide causal-process evidence on how policy *contexts* alter developmental trajectories. It will directly inform the policy roadmap for reducing child poverty by identifying which macro-scale policy levers—cash transfers, childcare subsidies, or universal services—are most potent in breaking the link between deprivation and key life outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While comparative policy research exists, few studies integrate detailed longitudinal child development data from multiple countries into a formal statistical test of policy moderation. This approach moves from descriptive regime typology to a direct, quantitative test of how policy architectures alter a well-established micro-level developmental pathway.

proposals.title: The Child-to-Parent Economic Spillover: Estimating the Causal Impact of Early Childhood Developmental Problems on Parental Labor Market Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Extensive research, including Duncan's own work, examines how parental poverty and employment affect child development and well-being. However, the inverse pathway—where a child's developmental problems (e.g., cognitive delays, behavioral issues) impact parental economic outcomes—is understudied. Understanding this bidirectional relationship is crucial for a complete model of intergenerational poverty and for designing effective family support policies, especially in the context of post-pandemic economic recovery and rising concerns about child mental health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the onset of significant developmental problems in early childhood (ages 3-7) causes a measurable reduction in maternal employment probability and household income, with effects concentrated among families with lower socioeconomic resources and less access to formal childcare or support services.
proposals.method: We will use longitudinal data from the Fragile Families and Child Wellbeing Study and the Panel Study of Income Dynamics-Child Development Supplement. Applying a sibling fixed-effects model, we will compare economic outcomes for the same parent before and after a developmental problem is identified in one child, using their sibling(s) as a control. To strengthen causal inference, we will employ an instrumental variables approach, using plausibly exogenous variation in the severity of a child's condition (e.g., diagnosis timing, genetic risk scores available in some cohorts) as an instrument for the treatment. Analyses will control for time-varying household confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first robust, causal estimates of child-driven economic penalties on parents, quantifying a previously overlooked mechanism in the cycle of disadvantage. It will directly inform economic models of human capital development and policy debates on paid family leave, childcare subsidies, and disability support by highlighting the economic vulnerability of families caring for children with special needs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work notes correlations, this proposal innovates by rigorously testing the *causal direction* from child problems to parent economics, using family fixed-effects and IV strategies to address the profound confounding inherent in this relationship, moving beyond the established paradigm of parent effects on children.

proposals.title: Beyond Income: Testing the Effects of Residential Mobility on Wealth Accumulation and Intergenerational Mobility
proposals.motivation: Prior research, including Moving to Opportunity (MTO) and Duncan's own work on poverty, shows that moving to lower-poverty neighborhoods can improve children's long-term educational attainment and health, but has limited impact on adult economic self-sufficiency as measured by earnings. This leaves a critical gap: economic well-being is multi-dimensional, encompassing wealth (assets minus debts) and intergenerational progress, which are crucial for long-term security and escaping poverty traps. Understanding whether residential moves affect these broader economic constructs is essential for a complete policy evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that moving to a lower-poverty area during childhood, while not strongly affecting later earned income, will positively influence alternative measures of economic well-being, specifically leading to greater wealth accumulation in adulthood and improved measures of intergenerational economic mobility compared to children who did not move.
proposals.method: We will leverage long-term follow-up data from the Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment and other quasi-experimental mobility studies, merging them with administrative data on assets, debts, and credit scores (e.g., from the FRBNY Consumer Credit Panel/Equifax). We will employ intent-to-treat and compiler-average causal effect analyses using the original random assignment as an instrument. For intergenerational mobility, we will compare the adult economic outcomes of the children of the original MTO participants to their parents' baseline status, using rank-rank slope estimations to measure mobility.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence on whether neighborhood environments causally affect wealth building and intergenerational economic progress. It will shift the policy conversation from a narrow focus on income to a broader conception of economic well-being. The findings will directly inform anti-poverty strategies, such as housing mobility programs, by identifying their potential to disrupt the intergenerational transmission of poverty through channels beyond labor markets.
proposals.novelty: While the null effect on earnings is established, it is non-obvious whether the documented gains in education and health from moving translate into the financial capital and intergenerational advancement captured by wealth and mobility metrics, which are distinct from income.

proposals.title: The Influx Effect: Child Well-Being and Educational Attainment in Lower-Poverty Neighborhoods Receiving Low-Income Newcomers
proposals.motivation: Research on neighborhood effects, including Duncan's work, has focused on moving low-income families to lower-poverty areas. The reverse phenomenon—an influx of low-income newcomers into established, lower-poverty communities—is understudied. This 'dual problem' may strain local resources and social cohesion, potentially impacting the well-being and educational trajectories of long-term resident children, a critical gap in understanding the full dynamics of neighborhood change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an influx of low-income newcomers into lower-poverty neighborhoods will be associated with measurable declines in the academic performance and non-cognitive skill development of incumbent, low-income children, mediated by changes in school quality, peer environments, and parental perceptions of neighborhood safety and social capital.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal, geocoded data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development Supplement (CDS), merged with Census and American Community Survey data to define neighborhood poverty transitions (2000-2025). Using a difference-in-differences framework, we will compare outcomes (test scores, behavioral indices, high school completion) for incumbent children in 'influx' neighborhoods versus matched stable neighborhoods. We will employ fixed-effects models to control for time-invariant family characteristics and mediation analysis (e.g., Baron & Kenny steps, structural equation modeling) to test pathways via school-level administrative data and parental survey reports.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, longitudinal evidence on how neighborhood poverty influx affects incumbent children, directly complementing Moving to Opportunity findings. It will advance the field by modeling neighborhood change as a dynamic, two-way process and identify specific mediators, offering policymakers insights into mitigating potential negative consequences of demographic shifts for all vulnerable children.
proposals.novelty: It flips the classic neighborhood effects question to examine impacts on those 'left behind' in improving areas, moving beyond a simple gentrification-displacement frame to analyze the nuanced effects of increased economic diversity within lower-poverty contexts.

proposals.title: Generalizing the Impacts of Residential Mobility: A Quasi-Experimental Test of Neighborhood Effects Using Administrative Data on Compulsory Relocations
proposals.motivation: Key evidence on neighborhood effects on child development, such as from the Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment, relies on families who volunteered for housing mobility programs. This self-selection raises critical questions about the external validity of these findings for the broader population of low-income families, many of whom move under duress or due to forced displacement. Understanding whether beneficial effects generalize to non-volunteer families is essential for informing scalable anti-poverty and housing policies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive impacts of moving to lower-poverty neighborhoods on children's long-term educational attainment and health outcomes (e.g., reduced obesity) will be significantly attenuated or absent for families compelled to move through non-voluntary mechanisms, compared to the volunteer samples studied in prior experimental work.
proposals.method: We will leverage linked, longitudinal administrative data (pre-2026) tracking families involved in compulsory relocations due to public housing demolitions, natural disasters, or court-ordered building condemnations. Using a matched difference-in-differences design, we will compare children in these 'compelled mover' families to a statistically matched control group of children in similar, non-relocated buildings. Outcomes will include school district records on educational attainment and Medicaid claims data on obesity diagnoses. We will employ propensity score matching and fixed-effects regression models to control for pre-move characteristics and isolate the effect of the move itself.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, quasi-experimental evidence on the external validity of neighborhood effects for non-volunteer populations. It will directly inform the 'scaling up' debate in poverty policy by clarifying for whom residential mobility programs are likely to be effective. The findings will contribute to a more nuanced understanding of the interaction between family agency, stress, and environmental change in shaping child well-being.
proposals.novelty: While prior work critiques the self-selected nature of mobility program participants, no study has systematically tested for effect heterogeneity using a counterfactual design applied to a population of families who moved under compulsion, a common yet understudied experience in low-income communities.

proposals.title: Understanding Non-Participation: A Mixed-Methods Study of Families Who Qualified for but Declined a Housing Mobility Program
proposals.motivation: The Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment and subsequent housing mobility programs have demonstrated significant, if complex, impacts on children's long-term economic and educational attainment. However, program efficacy is limited by take-up rates. Understanding why eligible families decline participation is crucial for designing more effective anti-poverty policies and for assessing the generalizability of experimental findings, as non-participants may differ systematically from participants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that families who decline participation are not a random subset of the eligible population but are systematically different in their perceptions of neighborhood quality, social capital, and risk aversion, and that these differences are associated with distinct pre-existing trajectories in child well-being and educational engagement.
proposals.method: We will leverage administrative and survey data from a recent mobility program (e.g., a HUD demonstration) to identify qualified non-participants. Using a matched cohort design, we will apply propensity score matching to pair declining families with participating families on pre-program observables (income, family structure, baseline child outcomes). We will then employ logistic regression and qualitative comparative analysis (QCA) on follow-up survey data to model the decision to decline, focusing on perceived barriers (e.g., moving costs, social ties, safety concerns) and their association with subsequent measures of child educational engagement and family economic well-being.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical evidence on the barriers to effective policy implementation, directly informing the 'roadmap to reducing child poverty.' It will advance methodological rigor in program evaluation by explicitly modeling selection into treatment, thereby clarifying the construct of who benefits from mobility interventions and under what conditions.
proposals.novelty: While vast literature analyzes MTO participants, a focused, mixed-methods investigation of the counterfactual group—those who declined the voucher—is novel. Inverting the focus from program impacts to the decision process itself addresses a key gap in understanding the limits and real-world applicability of mobility-based poverty interventions.

proposals.title: Intergenerational Mobility and the Legacy of Moving to Opportunity: Educational and Economic Outcomes for the Children of MTO Participants
proposals.motivation: The Moving to Opportunity (MTO) experiment demonstrated that moving from high-poverty to lower-poverty neighborhoods improved long-term economic and health outcomes for young children. However, a critical unanswered question is whether these benefits extend to the next generation. Understanding the intergenerational transmission of advantage is central to evaluating the full impact of place-based poverty alleviation policies and informing long-term investments in child well-being.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that children whose parents moved via the MTO program, particularly when those parents were young at the time of the move, will exhibit significantly better educational attainment (e.g., high school graduation, college enrollment) and early-adulthood economic outcomes (e.g., earnings, poverty status) compared to children of control group participants.
proposals.method: We will leverage the existing longitudinal MTO data, linking original participant records to administrative data (e.g., tax records, educational transcripts) for their children. The core analysis will employ an intent-to-treat framework, comparing outcomes for children based on their parent's original random assignment. We will use regression models with robust standard errors, controlling for parent and child demographics. Subgroup analyses will test for effect heterogeneity by the child's sex, the parent's age at move, and the destination neighborhood's characteristics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous evidence on whether the benefits of a major housing mobility program cascade to a second generation. It will directly inform policy debates on the long-term cost-effectiveness of housing interventions and contribute to developmental science by elucidating the mechanisms of intergenerational mobility.
proposals.novelty: While prior MTO research focused on the original participants, no study has systematically analyzed the life outcomes of their children using the experiment's randomization. This moves beyond the individual lifetime to assess the program's legacy, a novel and policy-relevant extension of the classic design.

proposals.title: The Sticky Neighborhood: Unpacking the Social and Economic Mechanisms of Persistence in Community Quality
proposals.motivation: Neighborhood quality is a powerful determinant of child development and long-term life outcomes, including educational attainment and health. However, we lack a clear understanding of why some neighborhoods remain high- or low-quality over generations, perpetuating cycles of advantage or disadvantage. Grounded in pre-2026 work on poverty and child well-being, this research addresses a critical gap in explaining the persistence of place-based inequality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sustained neighborhood quality is primarily driven by a combination of (1) the intergenerational transmission of economic resources and housing tenure, and (2) self-reinforcing social networks and collective efficacy that maintain local norms and institutional quality.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal data (e.g., PSID, NLSY) with geocoded addresses to model neighborhood trajectory classes (e.g., persistently high, declining, improving) using latent class growth analysis. We will then employ a series of mediation and structural equation models to test the relative strength of hypothesized mechanisms—including wealth accumulation, residential stability, social cohesion, and access to quality schools—in explaining these trajectories, controlling for individual and metropolitan-level confounders.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond documenting neighborhood effects to explicitly model the processes that sustain them. It will provide actionable evidence for policymakers seeking to disrupt negative cycles of neighborhood disadvantage, informing place-based interventions and housing policies aimed at increasing mobility and opportunity.
proposals.novelty: While prior research links neighborhoods to outcomes, this study uniquely focuses on the dynamic *persistence* of neighborhood quality itself, integrating economic transmission and social-psychological constructs into a unified analytical framework to explain stability versus change.

proposals.title: Micro-Geographic Mobility and Child Development: A Quasi-Experimental Study of Short-Distance Moves Within Cities
proposals.motivation: Decades of research, including Duncan's work on poverty and attainment, demonstrate that neighborhoods influence child development. However, large-scale studies like Moving to Opportunity (MTO) examine moves across broad neighborhood quality gradients, potentially conflating effects of social context with other factors like school district changes. The impact of moving just a few blocks within the same broader community remains a critical, understudied micro-geographic question with direct relevance for housing policy and urban planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among low-income families, moving a short distance (e.g., <1 mile) to a census block group with a higher socioeconomic status (SES) index, while remaining within the same school catchment area, will lead to measurable improvements in children's non-cognitive skills and body mass index (BMI), independent of changes in school quality.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal, geocoded data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development Supplement (CDS), focusing on children who move within the same city. Using a propensity score matching approach, we will match movers to observably similar non-movers, controlling for pre-move household characteristics. The key independent variable is the change in a micro-neighborhood index (constructed from ACS data at the block group level for poverty, education, and unemployment) before and after the move. We will employ fixed-effects regression models to estimate the impact of this micro-geographic SES change on outcomes including behavioral problems, socio-emotional skills, and age- and sex-standardized BMI z-scores.
proposals.contribution: This study will isolate the 'pure' effect of residential social context from the confounding effect of school changes, a major limitation in prior neighborhood effects research. It will provide more precise, actionable evidence for policymakers on whether very localized investments and mixed-income housing developments can improve child well-being. The findings will directly inform debates on the mechanisms behind the success of place-based anti-poverty programs.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares different neighborhoods or cities, this study's innovation is its 'scale down' to examine hyper-local variation, using a quasi-experimental design to isolate the impact of social context from school quality—a key confound in studies of longer-distance moves.

proposals.title: Neighborhood-Level Spillovers of Anti-Poverty Programs: A Quasi-Experimental Analysis of Housing Mobility and Neighborhood Change
proposals.motivation: A major focus of policy research, including the 2019 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' report, is on the benefits of moving families from high-poverty to lower-poverty neighborhoods. However, the potential effects of such an influx on the receiving neighborhoods themselves are poorly understood. This creates a critical gap in evaluating the full social impact of mobility programs, as unintended consequences for neighborhood stability, resources, and social cohesion could undermine long-term benefits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rapid, program-induced influx of low-income families into a lower-poverty neighborhood will lead to measurable changes in that neighborhood's economic and social characteristics, including increased poverty concentration, residential turnover, and strain on local public services, potentially offsetting some individual-level gains for participants.
proposals.method: We will employ a difference-in-differences design, leveraging the quasi-random timing and location of housing voucher utilization from Moving to Opportunity (MTO) and subsequent program expansions. Using longitudinal census tract data (2000-2020 ACS) merged with HUD administrative data, we will model neighborhood change in treatment tracts (receiving clusters of program participants) versus matched control tracts. Key dependent variables will include tract poverty rate, median rent, school enrollment demographics, and crime rates. We will use entropy balancing to pre-process covariates and employ event-study specifications to trace dynamic effects.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first rigorous, large-scale evidence on the neighborhood-level spillover effects of concentrated housing mobility, moving beyond individual-centric evaluations. It will directly inform the design of housing assistance policies by quantifying potential trade-offs between participant well-being and community stability. The findings will contribute to a more holistic understanding of poverty deconcentration as a strategy.
proposals.novelty: While extensive research examines how neighborhoods affect individuals, this study flips the causal lens to ask how a targeted influx of program participants affects neighborhood ecologies—a dual problem largely absent from pre-2026 literature. It innovatively applies causal inference methods typically used for individual outcomes to the unit of the neighborhood.

proposals.title: The Incomplete Escape: Job Quality, Work Supports, and the Persistence of Poverty Among Former Welfare Recipients
proposals.motivation: Post-1996 welfare reform emphasized work as the primary route out of poverty, a policy stance reinforced by the 2019 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' report. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests that many who transition from welfare to work remain in or near poverty due to low wages, unstable schedules, and high costs. This research directly tests the core assumption of work-first policies, addressing a critical gap in understanding the economic well-being of families after they leave cash assistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a significant subset of former welfare recipients, securing employment is insufficient to lift a household above the poverty threshold, and that this insufficiency is systematically predicted by job quality characteristics (e.g., wage levels, benefits, schedule predictability) and the local cost of essential goods like housing and childcare.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal data from the Survey of Income and Program Participation (SIPP) and the Fragile Families and Child Wellbeing Study, tracking cohorts from the early 2000s onward. We will employ a dynamic multi-state model to analyze transitions between welfare receipt, employment in different quality quartiles, and poverty status. The analysis will control for human capital, local labor markets, and access to work supports (EITC, SNAP, childcare subsidies) using fixed-effects regression and propensity score matching to address confounding, building on Duncan's methodological rigor in causal inference.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a nuanced, longitudinal portrait of 'working poverty' post-welfare exit, quantifying the role of job quality versus mere employment. It will contribute directly to policy debates on the sufficiency of work-first strategies and inform the design of complementary supports, such as wage subsidies and childcare policy, aimed at genuine poverty reduction.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines employment rates or income after welfare, this study uniquely inverts the policy assumption by treating employment as a necessary but insufficient condition, making job quality and work supports the central explanatory constructs for poverty persistence.

proposals.title: Long-Term Impacts of Post-1996 Welfare Reform on Child and Adolescent Attainment, Obesity, and Well-Being: A Quasi-Experimental, Longitudinal Analysis
proposals.motivation: The 1996 Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity Reconciliation Act fundamentally restructured U.S. anti-poverty policy, shifting from cash assistance to work requirements and time limits. While short-term effects on maternal employment and income were studied, the long-term developmental consequences for the generation of children growing up under this new regime remain inadequately understood, particularly for educational attainment and health outcomes like obesity. This gap is critical for informing future poverty reduction strategies, as highlighted by the 2019 'Roadmap to Reducing Child Poverty' report.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that exposure to stricter welfare regimes during early childhood, mediated by changes in household economic resources, parental stress, and time use, has had significant negative long-term effects on educational attainment and positive effects on adolescent obesity rates, with impacts varying by the intensity of state-level policy implementation and family socioeconomic characteristics.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development and Transition to Adulthood supplements, creating a longitudinal cohort of children observed from the late 1990s through the 2020s. We will employ a difference-in-differences framework, exploiting variation in the timing and stringency of state-level welfare reforms post-1996. Key outcomes include high school completion, college enrollment, BMI trajectories, and subjective well-being. We will use fixed-effects models and mediation analysis (e.g., using the Karlson-Holm-Breen method) to test pathways through poverty depth, material hardship, and parental employment.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, nationally representative evidence on how a major shift in social policy shaped the life course of a generation across multiple domains of attainment and health. It will directly inform debates on the design of safety net programs by quantifying long-term trade-offs between parental employment mandates and child development. The findings will extend Duncan's prior work on poverty impacts by isolating the effects of a specific, consequential policy lever.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examined short-term effects, this study uniquely tracks children into adulthood, simultaneously modeling educational and physical health outcomes. The quasi-experimental design moves beyond correlational analysis to stronger causal inference regarding the enduring legacy of welfare reform, addressing construct clarity concerns similar to those in the 'marshmallow test' critiques by rigorously controlling for confounding household characteristics.

proposals.title: Local Labor Markets as Mediators: How County-Level Economic Conditions Shape the Child and Family Outcomes of Welfare Reform
proposals.motivation: The 1996 welfare reform (PRWORA) aimed to move families from welfare to work, but its impacts on child poverty and well-being were heterogeneous. Pre-2026 research, including Duncan's work, suggests that local economic contexts, such as job availability and wages, are critical yet understudied moderators of policy effectiveness. Understanding this mediation is essential for designing place-sensitive policies that genuinely improve child attainment and reduce obesity risks linked to economic stress.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the positive impacts of welfare reform on parental employment and, subsequently, on child educational attainment and health outcomes are significantly stronger in counties with tighter labor markets (lower unemployment, higher job growth) and higher wages in low-skill sectors.
proposals.method: We will leverage longitudinal data from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) and its Child Development Supplement, merged with county-level economic data from the Bureau of Labor Statistics and Quarterly Workforce Indicators. A difference-in-differences framework, comparing outcomes before and after reform across counties with varying economic conditions, will be employed. We will use causal mediation analysis with inverse probability weighting to formally decompose the total effect of reform into direct effects and indirect effects mediated through local job market conditions and parental employment.
